topic: API Authentication Status Code and Token Handling

critical_distinctions.name: Authentication Failure vs Authorization Failure
critical_distinctions.distinction: Authentication failure (identity not established or cannot be verified) versus authorization failure (identity established but access denied)

critical_distinctions.name: Expired Token Classification
critical_distinctions.distinction: Expired tokens are authentication failures, not authorization failures

critical_distinctions.name: Malformed Token Classification
critical_distinctions.distinction: Malformed tokens are authentication failures, not authorization failures

critical_distinctions.name: WWW-Authenticate Header Conditionality
critical_distinctions.distinction: WWW-Authenticate headers are required when the auth scheme requires client retry with credentials, not in all 401 responses unconditionally

critical_distinctions.name: Token Lifetime Security Posture
critical_distinctions.distinction: Short-lived tokens versus long-lived tokens as a security design choice

decision_rules.name: Status Code Selection Based on Failure Type
decision_rules.trigger: API request fails authentication or authorization check
decision_rules.preferred_action: Return 401 Unauthorized when caller has not authenticated OR their token is missing, invalid, or expired; Return 403 Forbidden when caller is authenticated but lacks permission
decision_rules.boundary_condition: The key distinction is whether identity has been established. If identity is unknown or cannot be verified (authentication failure), use 401. If identity is known but access is denied (authorization failure), use 403.

decision_rules.name: Expired Token Handling
decision_rules.preferred_action: Reject with 401 Unauthorized; Do NOT use 403 Forbidden
decision_rules.boundary_condition: This applies regardless of whether the token would have granted sufficient permissions if it were valid. The authentication layer must reject before authorization evaluation.

decision_rules.name: Malformed Token Handling
decision_rules.trigger: Received token cannot be parsed or validated
decision_rules.preferred_action: Reject with 401 Unauthorized; Do NOT use 403 Forbidden
decision_rules.boundary_condition: This includes tokens with invalid signatures, corrupted encoding, or structure that doesn't match the expected format.

decision_rules.name: WWW-Authenticate Header Requirements
decision_rules.preferred_action: Return WWW-Authenticate header when the auth scheme requires the client to retry with credentials
decision_rules.boundary_condition: The WWW-Authenticate header signals to the client what authentication scheme to use. Include this when you want the client to attempt authentication or re-authentication.

decision_rules.name: Token Lifetime Policy
decision_rules.preferred_action: Prefer short-lived access tokens; Reject expired tokens with 401
decision_rules.boundary_condition: Short-lived tokens reduce the window of opportunity if a token is compromised. The specific duration should balance security needs against user experience and system load.

anti_patterns.name: Using 403 For Authentication Failures
anti_patterns.problem: Using 403 Forbidden for expired tokens, malformed tokens, or missing credentials
anti_patterns.why_wrong: This conflates authentication failure (who are you?) with authorization failure (what can you do?). It prevents clients from understanding whether they need to re-authenticate or if they need different permissions.
anti_patterns.correct_approach: Use 401 Unauthorized for all authentication failures, including expired and malformed tokens.

anti_patterns.name: Omitting WWW-Authenticate Headers
anti_patterns.problem: Returning 401 responses without WWW-Authenticate headers when the auth scheme requires client retry
anti_patterns.why_wrong: Clients may not know what authentication scheme to use or how to construct valid credentials.
anti_patterns.correct_approach: Include WWW-Authenticate headers with 401 responses to guide client authentication behavior.

anti_patterns.name: Undocumented Authentication vs Authorization Semantics
anti_patterns.problem: Failing to document the difference between authentication failure and authorization failure in operator-facing guidance
anti_patterns.why_wrong: Operators and developers need to understand this distinction to properly diagnose issues, implement correct error handling, and maintain consistent API behavior.
anti_patterns.correct_approach: Document the difference explicitly in operator-facing guidance. Make it clear that authentication establishes identity and authorization determines permissions.

source_materials.description: API authentication status code guidance covering 401 vs 403 distinction and WWW-Authenticate header requirements
Token lifecycle guidance covering short-lived token preference, expired token handling, and authentication vs authorization failure semantics
